result:
All four are confirmed as real (non-macro) function calls. Note: cbm's graph cache for this project only contains `Function`/`DEFINES` nodes — no `CALLS` edges exist at all (verified via schema query), so I read the source directly for the call graph and used cbm only to confirm `ksys_read` exists as a node and that its outbound trace is empty (consistent with no CALLS edges being indexed).

## `ksys_read()` — `fs/read_write.c:602-619`

In source order, every function directly called in the body:

| Line | Call | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 604 | `fdget_pos(fd)` | Not a macro — `static inline struct fd fdget_pos(int fd)` in `include/linux/file.h:72`, which itself calls `__to_fd(__fdget_pos(fd))` (an extern function, defined `fs/file.c:1057`). |
| 608 | `file_ppos(f.file)` | Not a macro — `static inline loff_t *file_ppos(struct file *file)` defined locally just above, `fs/read_write.c:597`. |
| 613 | `vfs_read(f.file, buf, count, ppos)` | Ordinary extern function, defined `fs/read_write.c:450`, declared `include/linux/fs.h:1964`. |
| 616 | `fdput_pos(f)` | Not a macro — `static inline void fdput_pos(struct fd f)` in `include/linux/file.h:77`, which internally calls `fdput(f)` (another static inline, `include/linux/file.h:43`) that may call `fput(fd.file)`. |

No macros are used for any of the four call sites — all are ordinary (or `static inline`) C function calls, so nothing needed macro-expansion resolution. The only macro-like symbol nearby is `FMODE_STREAM` (used in `file_ppos`, not in `ksys_read` itself) and `-EBADF`, neither of which is a call.
